architecture.summary: Grid Beam Search (GBS) extends standard beam search by organizing hypotheses in a 2D grid indexed by timestep and constraint-token coverage. At each step, hypotheses can generate freely from the model distribution, start new constraints, or continue incomplete constraints. The algorithm maintains 'open' hypotheses (which can generate or start constraints) and 'closed' hypotheses (which continue constraints). Beams at the top level (all constraints covered) produce final output. The system is model-agnostic and requires no parameter modification or retraining of the underlying sequence model.

biblio.title: Lexically Constrained Decoding for Sequence Generation Using Grid Beam Search

claims.statement: In simulated interactive post-editing with Pick-Revise, GBS achieves over 20 BLEU points improvement across all language pairs over four editing cycles with strict constraints
Domain adaptation via automatically extracted terminology with GBS yields approximately 1.8 BLEU points improvement for EN-DE and 2.6 BLEU points for EN-FR on Autodesk Post-Editing Corpus
Domain adaptation for EN-PT yields 13.73 BLEU points improvement using automatically extracted terminology with GBS
GBS can incorporate arbitrary multi-token phrases and single tokens as lexical constraints simultaneously, without requiring model retraining or parameter modification
Constraints can be specified as single tokens, multi-word phrases, or discontinuous sequences with filters; GBS handles unknown tokens via subword segmentation without constraint-aware training

Runtime complexity of GBS is O(|V| × k × T × |C|) versus standard beam search O(|V| × k × T), where |C| is total constraint tokens; efficiency requires small beam sizes and is mitigated by parallelizability of independent column beams

Manual analysis shows that lexical constraints placed by GBS have global effects on translation quality, guiding the model away from initially low-scoring hypotheses toward outputs matching reference translations
GBS is model-agnostic and applicable to any sequence generation task (summarization, captioning, dialog, question-answering) where constraints can be specified at inference time
